lines.translation: Wang Peng, what are you doing?
Today is Gao Xiaoyin's birthday. We're having a dance party at her place tonight. Can you go?
Seven o'clock. We'll eat first, and after dinner we'll sing and dance.
Xiaoyin and her boyfriend, Xiaoyin's older female cousin, Bai Ying'ai, your younger sister Wang Hong, and I heard some of Xiaoyin's middle school classmates will be there too.
What birthday gift are you giving to Xiaoyin?
I bought a book to give to her.
Beverages or fruit would be fine.
Then I'll bring some beverages, and also buy a bouquet of flowers.
Xiaoyin loves fruit. I'll buy some apples, pears, and a watermelon.
Your place is very far from Xiaoyin's house, and the fruit will be heavy. I'll drive to pick you up. Let's go together.
Great, I'll wait for you downstairs at six-thirty.

language_notes.label: a
language_notes.explanation_en: Apart from 过生日 (guò shēngrì) (to celebrate one's birthday), the verb 过 (guò) (to live [a life], to observe [a holiday], to celebrate [a festival]) appears in many other expressions, such as 过年 (guò nián) (to celebrate the New Year), 过节 (guò jié) (to celebrate a festival), and 过日子 (guò rìzi) (to live one's life, to live from day to day).

language_notes.label: b
language_notes.explanation_en: The kinship term 表姐 (biǎojiě) is more narrowly defined than its translation "older female cousin" would suggest. Your "older female cousin” is a 表姐 (biǎojiě) if she is a daughter of your father's sister or your mother's sister or brother. But if she is your paternal uncle's daughter, she is a 堂姐 (tángjiě), not a 表姐 (biǎojiě). Therefore, your 堂姐 (tángjiě) typically shares your family name. For more Chinese kinship terms, see Grammar 5 in Lesson 20.

grammar_sections.title: Indicating an action in progress using 呢 (ne)
grammar_sections.explanation_en: 呢 (ne) at the end of a sentence indicates that the action denoted by the verb is in progress. In this sense, it is similar to 在 (zài). However, 在 (zài) is not used at the end of a sentence, but before the verb. 呢 (ne) can also be used in conjunction with 在 (zài). 在 (zài) by itself can indicate that an action is in progress; therefore, the 呢 (ne) can be omitted. 在 (zài) can also be preceded by 正 (zhèng). The phrase 正在 (zhèngzài) further emphasizes that the action is ongoing.

patterns.meaning_en: Indicates an action in progress, can be used with 在 (zài).
Emphasizes that an action is ongoing.

examples.hanzi: 我昨天给他打电话的时候，他正在做功课呢。
examples.pinyin: Wǒ zuótiān gěi tā dǎ diànhuà de shíhou, tā zhèngzài zuò gōngkè ne.
examples.translation: When I called him yesterday, he was right in the middle of doing his homework.

examples.hanzi: 别去找他，他正在睡觉呢。
examples.pinyin: Bié qù zhǎo tā, tā zhèngzài shuì jiào ne.
examples.translation: Don't go looking for him. He's sleeping at the moment.

usage_notes: 呢 (ne) is placed at the end of a sentence to indicate an action in progress.
It can be used in conjunction with 在 (zài).
When 在 (zài) is used by itself to indicate an action in progress, the 呢 (ne) at the end of the sentence can be omitted.
The phrase 正在 (zhèngzài) can be used to further emphasize that the action is ongoing.

grammar_sections.title: Verbal phrases and subject-predicate phrases used as attributives
grammar_sections.explanation_en: In Chinese, attributives, often followed by the particle 的 (de), always appear before what they modify. Verbs, verbal phrases, and subject-predicate phrases can all serve as attributives.

patterns.meaning_en: A verbal phrase or a subject-predicate phrase functions as an adjective to modify a noun, connected by the particle 的 (de).

examples.translation: clothes to wear, or clothes being worn
friend/friends one previously got to know
the tofu dish my mother makes/made
the homework the teacher assigned us

examples.pinyin: qǐng nǐ tiào wǔ de nà gè rén
examples.translation: that person who asked you to dance

examples.hanzi: 我妹妹喜欢去的那个很酷的地方
examples.pinyin: wǒ mèimei xǐhuan qù de nà ge hěn kù de dìfang
examples.translation: that very cool place that my younger sister loves to go to

language_practice_sections.title: A. In the midst
language_practice_sections.focus_pattern: Q: Subject + (在) + 做什么呢? A: Subject + (在) + Verb Phrase + 呢。

language_practice_sections.title: B. Oh, that one!
language_practice_sections.focus_pattern: Combine the two short sentences into one, using the highlighted verb and phrase to form an attributive. Add 的 (de) where appropriate.

drill_sentences.cn: 他写了一个字。那个字很漂亮。
drill_sentences.py: Tā xiě le yí ge zì. Nà ge zì hěn piàoliang.
drill_sentences.translation: He wrote a character. That character is very pretty.

drill_sentences.cn: 她买了一件衬衫。那件衬衫是中号的。
drill_sentences.py: Tā mǎi le yí jiàn chènshān. Nà jiàn chènshān shì zhōng hào de.
drill_sentences.translation: She bought a shirt. That shirt is a medium size.

drill_sentences.cn: 我哥哥给了我一枝笔。那枝笔是黑色的。
drill_sentences.py: Wǒ gēge gěi le wǒ yì zhī bǐ. Nà zhī bǐ shì hēisè de.
drill_sentences.translation: My older brother gave me a pen. That pen is black.

drill_sentences.cn: 妹妹带了一些水果。那些水果很贵。
drill_sentences.py: Mèimei dài le yì xiē shuǐguǒ. Nà xiē shuǐguǒ hěn guì.
drill_sentences.translation: Younger sister brought some fruit. That fruit is very expensive.

drill_sentences.cn: 表姐卖了一些花。那些花很漂亮。
drill_sentences.py: Biǎojiě mài le yì xiē huā. Nà xiē huā hěn piàoliang.
drill_sentences.translation: Older female cousin sold some flowers. Those flowers are very pretty.

language_practice_sections.title: C. Who's who?
language_practice_sections.focus_pattern: Using verb phrases or subject-predicate phrases as attributives to identify people, e.g., 那个 + [Verb Phrase] + 的 + [Noun] + 是谁？

drill_sentences.cn: A: Jamal是那个穿蓝色衬衫的学生。
drill_sentences.py: A: Jamal shì nà ge chuān lánsè chènshān de xuésheng.
drill_sentences.translation: A: Jamal is that student wearing the blue shirt.

drill_sentences.cn: A: Katy是那个正在看书的女孩。
drill_sentences.py: A: Katy shì nà ge zhèngzài kàn shū de nǚhái.
drill_sentences.translation: A: Katy is that girl who is reading a book.

drill_sentences.cn: Q: 那个穿蓝色衬衫的学生是谁？
drill_sentences.py: Q: Nà ge chuān lánsè chènshān de xuésheng shì shéi?
drill_sentences.translation: Q: Who is that student wearing the blue shirt?

drill_sentences.cn: A: 那个穿蓝色衬衫的学生是Jamal。
drill_sentences.py: A: Nà ge chuān lánsè chènshān de xuésheng shì Jamal.
drill_sentences.translation: A: That student wearing the blue shirt is Jamal.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand how to ask what someone is currently doing using '做什么呢'.

narration.subtitle_en: The dialogue begins with Li You asking Wang Peng a common question: [zh:你做什么呢]. Let's break it down. [zh:你] means 'you'. [zh:做] means 'to do', and [zh:什么] means 'what'. The particle [zh:呢] at the end of the sentence is key here. It indicates that the action is currently in progress. So, Li You is asking, 'What are you doing right now?'

on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Wáng Péng, nǐ zuò shénme ne?
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Wang Peng, what are you doing?
on_screen_text.notes: The particle '呢' (ne) indicates an ongoing action.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A versatile verb meaning 'to do', 'to make', or 'to be'.
A common question word used to ask 'what'.
A sentence-final particle that indicates an action is in progress.

segments.visual_notes: Animate the characters 做, 什么, and 呢 as they are explained.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to describe what you are currently doing.

narration.subtitle_en: Wang Peng replies, [zh:我看书呢]. This follows the same structure. [zh:我] is 'I'. [zh:看书] is a common phrase meaning 'to read a book,' where [zh:看] is 'to look' or 'to read,' and [zh:书] is 'book.' He also uses the particle [zh:呢] at the end to confirm that this is what he is doing right now. It's a direct and simple answer to Li You's question.

on_screen_text.notes: '看书' (kàn shū) literally means 'look at book'.

highlight_words.translation: to read, to see, to watch
highlight_words.explanation_en: A verb for actions involving sight, such as reading, watching TV, or seeing something.
The noun for 'book'.

segments.visual_notes: Show an icon of a person reading a book. Highlight the '呢' particle again to reinforce its function.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to invite someone to a party and ask if they can attend.

narration.subtitle_en: Now, Li You gets to the main point. This is a long sentence, so let's break it into parts. First, [zh:今天高小音过生日] means 'Today is Gao Xiaoyin's birthday.' The verb [zh:过] is used for celebrating holidays or birthdays. Next, [zh:晚上我们在她家开舞会] means 'Tonight, we're having a dance party at her house.' [zh:舞会] is a dance party. Finally, she asks, [zh:你能去吗], which means 'Can you go?'. This is a polite way to invite someone.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 今天高小音过生日，晚上我们在她家开舞会，你能去吗？
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: jīntiān gāo xiǎoyīn guò shēngrì, wǎnshang wǒmen zài tā jiā kāi wǔhuì, nǐ néng qù ma?
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Today is Gao Xiaoyin's birthday. We're having a dance party at her place tonight. Can you go?

highlight_words.explanation_en: The verb '过' (guò) means to pass or to celebrate, and '生日' (shēngrì) is birthday.
Composed of '舞' (wǔ) meaning 'dance' and '会' (huì) meaning 'gathering' or 'party'.
An auxiliary verb indicating ability or permission.

segments.visual_notes: Break the sentence into three visual chunks on screen as each part is explained.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to accept an invitation and ask for the time.

narration.subtitle_en: Wang Peng's response is short and to the point. He says [zh:能去], which means 'I can go.' This is a direct, positive answer to Li You's question. Immediately after, he asks for a key detail: [zh:几点]. This means 'What time?'. The word [zh:几] is used to ask 'how many' for small numbers, and it's the standard way to ask for the hour.

on_screen_text.notes: '几点' (jǐ diǎn) is the common way to ask 'what time is it?'.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A simple and direct way to accept an invitation.
Literally 'how many o'clock', used to ask for the time.

segments.visual_notes: Show the two parts of the sentence separately to emphasize the two distinct actions: accepting and asking.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn the '先...再...' structure to describe a sequence of actions.

narration.subtitle_en: Li You provides the time, [zh:七点], 'seven o'clock.' Then she outlines the party's schedule using a very important pattern: [zh:先...再...], which means 'first... then...'. She says, [zh:我们先吃饭], 'we'll eat first,' followed by [zh:吃完饭再唱歌跳舞], 'after finishing the meal, then we'll sing and dance.' This structure is perfect for describing plans in order.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 七点。我们先吃饭，吃完饭再唱歌跳舞。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: qī diǎn. wǒmen xiān chīfàn, chī wán fàn zài chànggē tiàowǔ.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Seven o'clock. We'll eat first, and after dinner we'll sing and dance.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Indicates the first action in a sequence.
Indicates the subsequent action in a sequence.
Literally 'sing song'.
Literally 'jump dance'.

segments.visual_notes: Highlight '先' and '再' in different colors to emphasize the sequential structure.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to ask about the attendees of an event.

narration.subtitle_en: Wang Peng is curious about the other guests. He asks, [zh:有哪些人]. This is a useful phrase that means 'Who will be there?' Let's look at the words. [zh:有] means 'there are,' [zh:哪些] means 'which ones' for plural items or people, and [zh:人] means 'people.' So, literally, he's asking, 'There are which people?'

on_screen_text.notes: '哪些' (nǎxiē) is the plural form of 'which' (哪, nǎ).

highlight_words.explanation_en: Indicates existence or possession.
A question word used to ask 'which' when the answer is expected to be more than one.

segments.visual_notes: Show icons of people with question marks above them.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand vocabulary for different relationships and how to list people.

narration.subtitle_en: Li You lists the guests. We hear several relationship terms. [zh:男朋友] is 'boyfriend.' [zh:表姐] is 'older female cousin.' It's important to know that Chinese kinship terms are very specific. A [zh:表姐] is a cousin from your mother's side or your father's sister. A cousin from your father's brother would be a [zh:堂姐]. We also hear [zh:妹妹], 'younger sister,' and [zh:中学同学], 'middle school classmates.' The phrase [zh:听说] means 'it is said' or 'I heard that.'

on_screen_text.focus_text: 小音和她的男朋友、小音的表姐、白英爱、你妹妹王红，听说还有小音的中学同学。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: xiǎo yīn hé tā de nán péng you, xiǎo yīn de biǎo jiě, bái yīng ài, nǐ mèi mei wáng hóng, tīng shuō hái yǒu xiǎo yīn de zhōng xué tóng xué.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Xiaoyin and her boyfriend, Xiaoyin's older female cousin, Bai Ying'ai, your younger sister Wang Hong, and I heard some of Xiaoyin's middle school classmates will be there too.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Literally 'male friend'.

highlight_words.translation: older female cousin (maternal or paternal aunt's daughter)
highlight_words.explanation_en: A specific term for an older female cousin from the maternal side or from a paternal aunt.
Composed of '中' (zhōng) for 'middle' and '学' (xué) for 'school'.
Composed of '同' (tóng) for 'same' and '学' (xué) for 'study'.

segments.visual_notes: As each relationship term is mentioned, show a simple icon representing it (e.g., two girls for 'cousin').

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to ask what gift someone is planning to give.

narration.subtitle_en: Now the conversation turns to gifts. Wang Peng asks, [zh:你要送给小音什么生日礼物]. Let's analyze this. [zh:你要] means 'you are going to'. The verb phrase [zh:送给] means 'to give to someone as a gift'. And the object is [zh:什么生日礼物], 'what birthday gift'. [zh:礼物] means 'gift' or 'present'.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 你要送给小音什么生日礼物？
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: nǐ yào sòng gěi xiǎo yīn shén me shēng rì lǐ wù?
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: What birthday gift are you giving to Xiaoyin?

highlight_words.explanation_en: This verb specifically means to give something as a present.
The general word for a gift or present.

segments.visual_notes: Show an icon of a gift box with a question mark.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to state what gift you bought and introduce the measure word '本'.

narration.subtitle_en: Li You reveals her gift: [zh:我买了一本书送给她]. This means 'I bought a book to give to her.' The first part is [zh:我买了一本书], 'I bought a book.' Pay attention to the word [zh:本]. This is a measure word, which is required in Chinese between a number and a noun. [zh:本] is specifically used for bound items like books or magazines.

on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: wǒ mǎi le yī běn shū sòng gěi tā.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: I bought a book to give to her.
on_screen_text.notes: '本' (běn) is the measure word for books.

highlight_words.explanation_en: The verb for purchasing something.
A classifier used for books, magazines, and other bound volumes.

segments.visual_notes: Animate the structure '一 + 本 + 书' to highlight the measure word.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to ask what you should bring to a party.

narration.subtitle_en: Hearing Li You's gift idea, Wang Peng asks for a suggestion for himself. He says, [zh:那我带什么东西]. The word [zh:那] at the beginning means 'then' or 'in that case.' [zh:我带] means 'I bring.' The verb [zh:带] means to bring or to take with. [zh:什么东西] means 'what things?' So, he's asking, 'Then what should I bring?'

highlight_words.explanation_en: The verb for bringing or carrying something with you.
A general noun for 'things' or 'stuff'. It literally means 'east-west'.

segments.visual_notes: Show an icon of a person shrugging with a question mark.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to give suggestions using '或者' and '都可以'.

narration.subtitle_en: Li You gives some easy suggestions: [zh:饮料或者水果都可以]. [zh:饮料] means 'beverages.' [zh:水果] is 'fruit.' The word [zh:或者] means 'or' and is typically used in statements, not questions. The final phrase, [zh:都可以], is very useful. It means 'all are okay' or 'either would be fine,' showing that any of the options are acceptable.

on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Beverages or fruit would be fine.

highlight_words.explanation_en: The general term for drinks.
A conjunction used to connect choices in a statement.
Literally 'water fruit'.

segments.visual_notes: Show an icon for 'beverages' and 'fruit' with 'OR' in between, leading to a thumbs-up icon for '都可以'.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to state what you will bring, using '一些' and the measure word '把'.

narration.subtitle_en: Wang Peng decides what to bring. He says, [zh:那我带一些饮料], 'Then I'll bring some beverages.' [zh:一些] means 'some.' He adds, [zh:再买一把花儿], 'and also buy a bouquet of flowers.' Here, [zh:再] means 'in addition.' We also see another measure word, [zh:把], which is used for a handful of something or things with a handle. A 'bunch' or 'bouquet' of flowers uses [zh:把].

on_screen_text.focus_text: 那我带一些饮料，再买一把花儿。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: nà wǒ dài yìxiē yǐnliào, zài mǎi yì bǎ huār.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Then I'll bring some beverages, and also buy a bouquet of flowers.
on_screen_text.notes: '把' (bǎ) is a measure word for a handful or a bunch.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Indicates an indefinite quantity.
A classifier for objects that can be held, or for a handful/bunch of something.
The noun for flower. The 'er' sound at the end is common in northern Chinese dialects.

segments.visual_notes: Animate the structure '一 + 把 + 花' to highlight the measure word.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn vocabulary for common fruits and the verb '爱'.

narration.subtitle_en: Li You has another idea. She starts with [zh:小音爱吃水果], 'Xiaoyin loves to eat fruit.' The verb [zh:爱] means 'to love' and can express a strong liking for an activity. Then she says, [zh:我再买一些...], 'I'll also buy some...' and lists several fruits: [zh:苹果] 'apples,' [zh:梨] 'pears,' and [zh:西瓜] 'watermelon.'

on_screen_text.focus_text: 小音爱吃水果，我再买一些苹果、梨和西瓜吧。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: xiǎo yīn ài chī shuǐ guǒ, wǒ zài mǎi yī xiē píng guǒ, lí hé xī guā ba.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Xiaoyin loves fruit. I'll buy some apples, pears, and a watermelon.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A verb expressing love or a strong fondness for something or someone.
The noun for apple.
The noun for pear.
Literally 'west melon'.

segments.visual_notes: Show icons for each fruit as it is named.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn phrases for offering help, like driving and picking someone up.

narration.subtitle_en: Wang Peng makes a very considerate offer. He gives his reasons first: [zh:你住的地方离小音家很远], 'The place you live is far from Xiaoyin's house,' and [zh:水果很重], 'the fruit is heavy.' Then comes the offer: [zh:我开车去接你], 'I'll drive to pick you up.' [zh:开车] means 'to drive a car,' and [zh:接] means 'to pick someone up.' He finishes with a suggestion: [zh:我们一起去吧], 'Let's go together.'

on_screen_text.focus_text: 你住的地方离小音家很远，水果很重，我开车去接你，我们一起去吧。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: nǐ zhù de dì fang lí xiǎo yīn jiā hěn yuǎn, shuǐ guǒ hěn zhòng, wǒ kāi chē qù jiē nǐ, wǒ men yī qǐ qù ba.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Your place is very far from Xiaoyin's house, and the fruit will be heavy. I'll drive to pick you up. Let's go together.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A preposition used to talk about the distance between two places.
An adjective meaning 'far' or 'distant'.
An adjective meaning 'heavy'.
Literally 'to operate a car'.
A verb meaning to meet or welcome someone.

segments.visual_notes: Use icons to illustrate the concepts: a house, a long distance arrow, a heavy weight, a car, and two people together.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to agree to a plan and specify a time and meeting place.

narration.subtitle_en: The dialogue concludes with Li You accepting the offer and finalizing the details. She says [zh:好], 'Okay,' to agree. Then she specifies the time and place: [zh:我六点半在楼下等你]. This means 'I'll wait for you downstairs at 6:30.' [zh:六点半] is 'six-thirty,' as [zh:半] means 'half.' [zh:楼下] means 'downstairs,' and [zh:等] means 'to wait.' Now the plan is set!

on_screen_text.focus_text: 好，我六点半在楼下等你。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: hǎo, wǒ liù diǎn bàn zài lóu xià děng nǐ.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Great, I'll wait for you downstairs at six-thirty.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A common way to express time, meaning 'six and a half o'clock'.
Composed of '楼' (lóu) for 'building' and '下' (xià) for 'down'.
The verb for waiting.

segments.visual_notes: Show a clock pointing to 6:30 and an icon of a person waiting at the bottom of a building.

segments.segment_title: Grammar: Indicating an Action in Progress with 呢 (ne)
segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to use the particle 呢 (ne) to indicate that an action is currently happening.

narration.subtitle_en: In this lesson, we learn how to talk about actions that are happening right now. The particle [zh:呢] is placed at the end of a sentence to indicate this. For example, in the dialogue, Li You asks [zh:你做什么呢], 'What are you doing?'. Wang Peng replies, [zh:我看书呢], 'I'm reading a book.' This is very similar to using [zh:在] before the verb. You can even use them together for emphasis, like [zh:你在看书呢]. To further stress that the action is ongoing, you can use [zh:正在] before the verb, as in [zh:他正在做功课呢], 'He was right in the middle of doing his homework.'

on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Structure for indicating an action in progress.
on_screen_text.notes: 呢 (ne) at the end of a sentence indicates an ongoing action.

grammar_points.pattern: Q: 你做什么呢？ A: 我看书呢。
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: nǐ zuò shénme ne? wǒ kàn shū ne.
grammar_points.explanation_en: A common question and answer pattern to ask about and state an ongoing action, using 呢 (ne).
The preposition 在 (zài) can be used with 呢 (ne) to emphasize the ongoing nature of the action.
The adverb 正在 (zhèngzài) adds even more emphasis that the action is happening right at this moment.

segments.visual_notes: Animate the particle '呢' appearing at the end of the sentence. Show a character reading a book to illustrate '我看书呢'.

segments.segment_title: Grammar: Using Phrases as Attributives with 的 (de)
segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to use verbal phrases or subject-predicate phrases to describe a noun, connected by the particle 的 (de).

narration.subtitle_en: Next, let's look at how to describe nouns in more detail. In English, we often use clauses like 'the book that I bought'. In Chinese, this descriptive part always comes *before* the noun and is connected by the particle [zh:的]. For example, 'things to eat' becomes [zh:吃的东西]. 'The homework the teacher gave us' is [zh:老师给我们的功课]. Notice the structure: the description, then [zh:的], then the noun. In our dialogue, Wang Peng says [zh:你住的地方], which means 'the place where you live'. Here, [zh:你住] 'you live' describes [zh:地方] 'place'.

on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Structure for describing a noun with a phrase.
on_screen_text.notes: The descriptive phrase always precedes the noun it modifies.

grammar_points.explanation_en: A verbal phrase 'chī' (to eat) modifies the noun 'dōngxi' (things).
A subject-predicate phrase 'lǎoshī gěi wǒmen' (the teacher gives us) modifies 'gōngkè' (homework).
From the dialogue: 'nǐ zhù' (you live) modifies 'dìfang' (place).

grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: qǐng nǐ tiào wǔ de nà ge rén
grammar_points.explanation_en: A longer phrase 'qǐng nǐ tiào wǔ' (invites you to dance) modifies 'nà ge rén' (that person).

segments.visual_notes: Use color blocks to highlight the [Attributive Phrase], the [的], and the [Noun] to show how they fit together.

segments.segment_title: Language Notes: 过 (guò) and 表姐 (biǎojiě)
segments.teaching_goal: To understand the extended use of the verb 'guò' and the specific meaning of 'biǎojiě'.

narration.subtitle_en: Let's look at two cultural notes. First, the verb [zh:过]. In the dialogue, we see [zh:过生日], to celebrate a birthday. This verb is also used for other celebrations, like [zh:过年] for celebrating the New Year, or [zh:过节] for celebrating a festival. Second, let's clarify the term [zh:表姐]. While it translates to 'older female cousin,' it specifically refers to a cousin from your father's sister or your mother's side. If the cousin is from your father's brother, she would be your [zh:堂姐], and would usually share your last name. It's a more precise system than in English.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Used for celebrating birthdays (过生日), New Year (过年), and other festivals (过节).
Daughter of your father's sister or your mother's siblings.
Daughter of your father's brother. Usually shares your family name.

segments.visual_notes: Show a simple family tree diagram to illustrate the difference between 表姐 and 堂姐.

segments.teaching_goal: To practice asking what someone is doing and identifying people using descriptive phrases.

narration.subtitle_en: Let's practice these two grammar points together. To ask what someone is doing, you can use the pattern [zh:他做什么呢]. A possible answer is [zh:他在看书呢]. Now, let's use our second grammar point to identify that person. You could ask, [zh:那个正在看书的人是谁], 'Who is that person who is reading a book?'. This combines the ongoing action with the descriptive phrase structure. It's a powerful way to be more specific in your descriptions. Remember that the description must come before the noun.

on_screen_text.focus_text: Q: 那个正在看书的人是谁？ A: 他是王朋。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Q: nà ge zhèngzài kàn shū de rén shì shéi? A: tā shì wáng péng.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Q: Who is that person who is reading? A: He is Wang Peng.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A common question to ask about someone's current activity.
A structure to identify someone by their ongoing action.

segments.visual_notes: Show an animation where a question mark appears over a person reading, then the descriptive phrase highlights them, and finally their name tag appears.

segments.teaching_goal: To review the key grammar and vocabulary from the lesson.

narration.subtitle_en: Let's quickly review what we've learned. We covered how to indicate an ongoing action using [zh:呢] at the end of a sentence. We also learned the very important structure of using a phrase plus [zh:的] to describe a noun. Finally, we talked about sequences of actions with [zh:先...再...]. Keep practicing these patterns to make your Chinese more descriptive and fluent. Great job!

segments.teaching_goal: To learn the '先...再...' structure to describe a sequence of actions.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Li You provides the time, [zh:七点], 'seven o'clock.' Then she outlines the party's schedule using a very important pattern: [zh:先...再...], which means 'first... then...'. She says, [zh:我们先吃饭], 'we'll eat first,' followed by [zh:吃完饭再唱歌跳舞], 'after finishing the meal, then we'll sing and dance.' This structure is perfect for describing plans in order.

content.focus_text: 七点。我们先吃饭，吃完饭再唱歌跳舞。
content.focus_pinyin: qī diǎn. wǒmen xiān chīfàn, chī wán fàn zài chànggē tiàowǔ.
content.focus_gloss_en: Seven o'clock. We'll eat first, and after dinner we'll sing and dance.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Indicates the first action in a sequence.
Indicates the subsequent action in a sequence.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to ask about the attendees of an event.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Wang Peng is curious about the other guests. He asks, [zh:有哪些人]. This is a useful phrase that means 'Who will be there?' Let's look at the words. [zh:有] means 'there are,' [zh:哪些] means 'which ones' for plural items or people, and [zh:人] means 'people.' So, literally, he's asking, 'There are which people?'

content.notes: '哪些' (nǎxiē) is the plural form of 'which' (哪, nǎ).

highlight_words.explanation_en: Indicates existence or possession.
A question word used to ask 'which' when the answer is expected to be more than one.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand vocabulary for different relationships and how to list people.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Li You lists the guests. We hear several relationship terms. [zh:男朋友] is 'boyfriend.' [zh:表姐] is 'older female cousin.' It's important to know that Chinese kinship terms are very specific. A [zh:表姐] is a cousin from your mother's side or your father's sister. A cousin from your father's brother would be a [zh:堂姐]. We also hear [zh:妹妹], 'younger sister,' and [zh:中学同学], 'middle school classmates.' The phrase [zh:听说] means 'it is said' or 'I heard that.'

content.focus_text: 小音和她的男朋友、小音的表姐、白英爱、你妹妹王红，听说还有小音的中学同学。
content.focus_pinyin: xiǎo yīn hé tā de nán péng you, xiǎo yīn de biǎo jiě, bái yīng ài, nǐ mèi mei wáng hóng, tīng shuō hái yǒu xiǎo yīn de zhōng xué tóng xué.
content.focus_gloss_en: Xiaoyin and her boyfriend, Xiaoyin's older female cousin, Bai Ying'ai, your younger sister Wang Hong, and I heard some of Xiaoyin's middle school classmates will be there too.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to ask what gift someone is planning to give.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Now the conversation turns to gifts. Wang Peng asks, [zh:你要送给小音什么生日礼物]. Let's analyze this. [zh:你要] means 'you are going to'. The verb phrase [zh:送给] means 'to give to someone as a gift'. And the object is [zh:什么生日礼物], 'what birthday gift'. [zh:礼物] means 'gift' or 'present'.

content.focus_text: 你要送给小音什么生日礼物？
content.focus_pinyin: nǐ yào sòng gěi xiǎo yīn shén me shēng rì lǐ wù?
content.focus_gloss_en: What birthday gift are you giving to Xiaoyin?

highlight_words.explanation_en: This verb specifically means to give something as a present.
The general word for a gift or present.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to state what gift you bought and introduce the measure word '本'.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Li You reveals her gift: [zh:我买了一本书送给她]. This means 'I bought a book to give to her.' The first part is [zh:我买了一本书], 'I bought a book.' Pay attention to the word [zh:本]. This is a measure word, which is required in Chinese between a number and a noun. [zh:本] is specifically used for bound items like books or magazines.

content.focus_pinyin: wǒ mǎi le yī běn shū sòng gěi tā.
content.focus_gloss_en: I bought a book to give to her.

content.notes: '本' (běn) is the measure word for books.

highlight_words.explanation_en: The verb for purchasing something.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to ask what you should bring to a party.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Hearing Li You's gift idea, Wang Peng asks for a suggestion for himself. He says, [zh:那我带什么东西]. The word [zh:那] at the beginning means 'then' or 'in that case.' [zh:我带] means 'I bring.' The verb [zh:带] means to bring or to take with. [zh:什么东西] means 'what things?' So, he's asking, 'Then what should I bring?'

highlight_words.explanation_en: The verb for bringing or carrying something with you.
A general noun for 'things' or 'stuff'. It literally means 'east-west'.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to give suggestions using '或者' and '都可以'.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Li You gives some easy suggestions: [zh:饮料或者水果都可以]. [zh:饮料] means 'beverages.' [zh:水果] is 'fruit.' The word [zh:或者] means 'or' and is typically used in statements, not questions. The final phrase, [zh:都可以], is very useful. It means 'all are okay' or 'either would be fine,' showing that any of the options are acceptable.

content.focus_gloss_en: Beverages or fruit would be fine.

highlight_words.explanation_en: The general term for drinks.
A conjunction used to connect choices in a statement.
Literally 'water fruit'.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to state what you will bring, using '一些' and the measure word '把'.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Wang Peng decides what to bring. He says, [zh:那我带一些饮料], 'Then I'll bring some beverages.' [zh:一些] means 'some.' He adds, [zh:再买一把花儿], 'and also buy a bouquet of flowers.' Here, [zh:再] means 'in addition.' We also see another measure word, [zh:把], which is used for a handful of something or things with a handle. A 'bunch' or 'bouquet' of flowers uses [zh:把].

content.focus_text: 那我带一些饮料，再买一把花儿。
content.focus_pinyin: nà wǒ dài yìxiē yǐnliào, zài mǎi yì bǎ huār.
content.focus_gloss_en: Then I'll bring some beverages, and also buy a bouquet of flowers.

content.notes: '把' (bǎ) is a measure word for a handful or a bunch.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Indicates an indefinite quantity.
A classifier for objects that can be held, or for a handful/bunch of something.
The noun for flower. The 'er' sound at the end is common in northern Chinese dialects.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn vocabulary for common fruits and the verb '爱'.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Li You has another idea. She starts with [zh:小音爱吃水果], 'Xiaoyin loves to eat fruit.' The verb [zh:爱] means 'to love' and can express a strong liking for an activity. Then she says, [zh:我再买一些...], 'I'll also buy some...' and lists several fruits: [zh:苹果] 'apples,' [zh:梨] 'pears,' and [zh:西瓜] 'watermelon.'

content.focus_text: 小音爱吃水果，我再买一些苹果、梨和西瓜吧。

content.focus_gloss_en: Xiaoyin loves fruit. I'll buy some apples, pears, and a watermelon.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn phrases for offering help, like driving and picking someone up.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Wang Peng makes a very considerate offer. He gives his reasons first: [zh:你住的地方离小音家很远], 'The place you live is far from Xiaoyin's house,' and [zh:水果很重], 'the fruit is heavy.' Then comes the offer: [zh:我开车去接你], 'I'll drive to pick you up.' [zh:开车] means 'to drive a car,' and [zh:接] means 'to pick someone up.' He finishes with a suggestion: [zh:我们一起去吧], 'Let's go together.'

content.focus_text: 你住的地方离小音家很远，水果很重，我开车去接你，我们一起去吧。
content.focus_pinyin: nǐ zhù de dì fang lí xiǎo yīn jiā hěn yuǎn, shuǐ guǒ hěn zhòng, wǒ kāi chē qù jiē nǐ, wǒ men yī qǐ qù ba.
content.focus_gloss_en: Your place is very far from Xiaoyin's house, and the fruit will be heavy. I'll drive to pick you up. Let's go together.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to agree to a plan and specify a time and meeting place.

narration_track.subtitle_en: The dialogue concludes with Li You accepting the offer and finalizing the details. She says [zh:好], 'Okay,' to agree. Then she specifies the time and place: [zh:我六点半在楼下等你]. This means 'I'll wait for you downstairs at 6:30.' [zh:六点半] is 'six-thirty,' as [zh:半] means 'half.' [zh:楼下] means 'downstairs,' and [zh:等] means 'to wait.' Now the plan is set!

content.focus_text: 好，我六点半在楼下等你。
content.focus_pinyin: hǎo, wǒ liù diǎn bàn zài lóu xià děng nǐ.
content.focus_gloss_en: Great, I'll wait for you downstairs at six-thirty.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A common way to express time, meaning 'six and a half o'clock'.

segments.segment_title: Grammar: Indicating an Action in Progress with 呢 (ne)
segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to use the particle 呢 (ne) to indicate that an action is currently happening.

narration_track.subtitle_en: In this lesson, we learn how to talk about actions that are happening right now. The particle [zh:呢] is placed at the end of a sentence to indicate this. For example, in the dialogue, Li You asks [zh:你做什么呢], 'What are you doing?'. Wang Peng replies, [zh:我看书呢], 'I'm reading a book.' This is very similar to using [zh:在] before the verb. You can even use them together for emphasis, like [zh:你在看书呢]. To further stress that the action is ongoing, you can use [zh:正在] before the verb, as in [zh:他正在做功课呢], 'He was right in the middle of doing his homework.'

content.focus_gloss_en: Structure for indicating an action in progress.

grammar_points.pattern: Q: 你做什么呢？ A: 我看书呢。
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: nǐ zuò shénme ne? wǒ kàn shū ne.
grammar_points.explanation_en: A common question and answer pattern to ask about and state an ongoing action, using 呢 (ne).
The preposition 在 (zài) can be used with 呢 (ne) to emphasize the ongoing nature of the action.
The adverb 正在 (zhèngzài) adds even more emphasis that the action is happening right at this moment.

content.notes: 呢 (ne) at the end of a sentence indicates an ongoing action.

grammar_points.pattern: Q: 你做什么呢？ A: 我看书呢。
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: nǐ zuò shénme ne? wǒ kàn shū ne.
grammar_points.explanation_en: A common question and answer pattern to ask about and state an ongoing action, using 呢 (ne).
The preposition 在 (zài) can be used with 呢 (ne) to emphasize the ongoing nature of the action.
The adverb 正在 (zhèngzài) adds even more emphasis that the action is happening right at this moment.

segments.visual_notes: Animate the particle '呢' appearing at the end of the sentence. Show a character reading a book to illustrate '我看书呢'.

segments.segment_title: Grammar: Using Phrases as Attributives with 的 (de)
segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to use verbal phrases or subject-predicate phrases to describe a noun, connected by the particle 的 (de).

narration_track.subtitle_en: Next, let's look at how to describe nouns in more detail. In English, we often use clauses like 'the book that I bought'. In Chinese, this descriptive part always comes *before* the noun and is connected by the particle [zh:的]. For example, 'things to eat' becomes [zh:吃的东西]. 'The homework the teacher gave us' is [zh:老师给我们的功课]. Notice the structure: the description, then [zh:的], then the noun. In our dialogue, Wang Peng says [zh:你住的地方], which means 'the place where you live'. Here, [zh:你住] 'you live' describes [zh:地方] 'place'.

content.focus_gloss_en: Structure for describing a noun with a phrase.

grammar_points.explanation_en: A verbal phrase 'chī' (to eat) modifies the noun 'dōngxi' (things).
A subject-predicate phrase 'lǎoshī gěi wǒmen' (the teacher gives us) modifies 'gōngkè' (homework).
From the dialogue: 'nǐ zhù' (you live) modifies 'dìfang' (place).

grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: qǐng nǐ tiào wǔ de nà ge rén
grammar_points.explanation_en: A longer phrase 'qǐng nǐ tiào wǔ' (invites you to dance) modifies 'nà ge rén' (that person).

content.notes: The descriptive phrase always precedes the noun it modifies.

grammar_points.explanation_en: A verbal phrase 'chī' (to eat) modifies the noun 'dōngxi' (things).
A subject-predicate phrase 'lǎoshī gěi wǒmen' (the teacher gives us) modifies 'gōngkè' (homework).
From the dialogue: 'nǐ zhù' (you live) modifies 'dìfang' (place).

grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: qǐng nǐ tiào wǔ de nà ge rén
grammar_points.explanation_en: A longer phrase 'qǐng nǐ tiào wǔ' (invites you to dance) modifies 'nà ge rén' (that person).

segments.segment_title: Language Notes: 过 (guò) and 表姐 (biǎojiě)
segments.teaching_goal: To understand the extended use of the verb 'guò' and the specific meaning of 'biǎojiě'.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Let's look at two cultural notes. First, the verb [zh:过]. In the dialogue, we see [zh:过生日], to celebrate a birthday. This verb is also used for other celebrations, like [zh:过年] for celebrating the New Year, or [zh:过节] for celebrating a festival. Second, let's clarify the term [zh:表姐]. While it translates to 'older female cousin,' it specifically refers to a cousin from your father's sister or your mother's side. If the cousin is from your father's brother, she would be your [zh:堂姐], and would usually share your last name. It's a more precise system than in English.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Used for celebrating birthdays (过生日), New Year (过年), and other festivals (过节).
Daughter of your father's sister or your mother's siblings.
Daughter of your father's brother. Usually shares your family name.

segments.visual_notes: Show a simple family tree diagram to illustrate the difference between 表姐 and 堂姐.

segments.teaching_goal: To practice asking what someone is doing and identifying people using descriptive phrases.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Let's practice these two grammar points together. To ask what someone is doing, you can use the pattern [zh:他做什么呢]. A possible answer is [zh:他在看书呢]. Now, let's use our second grammar point to identify that person. You could ask, [zh:那个正在看书的人是谁], 'Who is that person who is reading a book?'. This combines the ongoing action with the descriptive phrase structure. It's a powerful way to be more specific in your descriptions. Remember that the description must come before the noun.

content.focus_text: Q: 那个正在看书的人是谁？ A: 他是王朋。
content.focus_pinyin: Q: nà ge zhèngzài kàn shū de rén shì shéi? A: tā shì wáng péng.
content.focus_gloss_en: Q: Who is that person who is reading? A: He is Wang Peng.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A common question to ask about someone's current activity.
A structure to identify someone by their ongoing action.

segments.visual_notes: Show an animation where a question mark appears over a person reading, then the descriptive phrase highlights them, and finally their name tag appears.

segments.teaching_goal: To review the key grammar and vocabulary from the lesson.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Let's quickly review what we've learned. We covered how to indicate an ongoing action using [zh:呢] at the end of a sentence. We also learned the very important structure of using a phrase plus [zh:的] to describe a noun. Finally, we talked about sequences of actions with [zh:先...再...]. Keep practicing these patterns to make your Chinese more descriptive and fluent. Great job!
